title: Flower Shaped Storage Play Table
summary: A kid-height play table with a scroll-cut flower-shaped top and a square storage box built into the base.
description:
The flower shape is laid out the way you'd fold a paper snowflake: find the center of the tabletop blank, mark a point 14" out along several reference lines, then draw an arch between each pair of points to form the petals. A single traced pattern, flipped and repeated around the circle, keeps every petal the same size — free-handing each one separately almost never comes out even.

Underneath, the storage box is ordinary pocket-hole construction: two leg-and-side subassemblies built first, then joined with the remaining two sides to close the box. Building it in that order — pairs first, then the whole — makes it much easier to keep everything square than trying to join four sides to four legs all at once.

tools.note: Cuts the curved flower-petal shape out of the tabletop.

materials.name: Pine, 1x3, 6 ft
materials.note: Substitute for plywood scraps on the storage-box aprons.

materials.name: Pine, 1x6, 6 ft
materials.note: Substitute for plywood scraps on the storage-box sides.

cutList.note: Cut the flower-petal shape into this blank in a later step.

steps.title: Break down the stock
steps.body: This can be built from plywood scraps for the storage box, or from the 1x6 and 1x3 boards as a substitute. When cutting the plywood, make long straight full-length cuts to conserve material: rip a strip for the 5 1/2"-wide box sides and a strip for the 2 1/2"-wide aprons down the length of the sheet, then cross-cut both strips into four pieces each at 15 3/4", and cut the 31 1/2" square tabletop blank and the 15 3/4" square box bottom from what is left. If you are using boards instead, the four 15 3/4" sides use 63" of the 6 ft 1x6 and the four 15 3/4" aprons use 63" of the 6 ft 1x3. Cut four legs at 20" from the 8 ft 2x2, which uses 80" of the board. Measure and cut as you go rather than marking every cut up front, since the blade's kerf eats into each mark.

steps.title: Lay out the flower shape
steps.body: Find the center of the 31 1/2" square blank by drawing both diagonals, then mark a point 14" out from that center along several reference lines radiating from it — a pencil tied to a string pinned at the center draws those points accurately. At a 14" radius the petal tips reach 28" across the 31 1/2" blank, which leaves about 1 3/4" of waste all around, so the layout has to be centered or you will run off the edge. Draw an arch between each neighboring pair of points to make the petals, and make a paper or cardboard template of one arch that you trace, flip and repeat around the circle — free-handing each petal separately almost never comes out even, and unevenness in a shape this simple is the only thing anyone will notice.

steps.title: Cut the flower shape
steps.body: Support the blank on sawhorses or scrap so the whole outline is clear underneath and nothing pinches the blade, then cut the petals out with the jigsaw, following just outside your line and letting the blade do the work through the turns. Cut the outside curve of each petal in one pass where you can; stopping and restarting mid-curve leaves a flat spot that is obvious once the top is finished. Use a fine-tooth or down-cut blade to hold the plywood's top veneer together at the cut. Sand the sawn edge fair afterward, checking the profile by eye from a few feet back, and ease the edge well — this is a kid's table and that edge is exactly at head height for a toddler.

steps.title: Build the storage box base
steps.body: Drill two 3/4"-setting pocket holes at each end of each 15 3/4" box side and attach them to the 20" legs with 1 1/4" pocket screws, flush to the insides of the legs so the leg faces stay proud. Build two leg-and-side subassemblies first — two legs joined by one side each — and only then join the remaining two sides to close the box; joining four sides to four legs all at once is where this goes out of square. Hold the sides up from the bottom of the legs so there is a 2" gap underneath for floor clearance, and use a scrap block cut to that height as a gauge under every leg rather than measuring four times. Check both diagonals across the closed box before the last screws go in.

steps.title: Attach the storage box bottom
steps.body: The 15 3/4" square plywood bottom is the same length as the sides, so it should drop into the opening between the legs with the sides' inside faces around it — if it will not go in, the box is out of square and now is when you fix that, not after the top is on. Slide it down flush with the bottom edges of the sides and fasten up through the sides into its edges, or set it on cleats if you would rather not see fasteners in the side faces. It doubles as the shelf everything sits on, so glue it as well as screwing it.

steps.title: Add the aprons
steps.body: The four 15 3/4" aprons sit on top of the base in the same footprint as the sides, tying the leg tops together and giving the tabletop something to land on across its whole width. Attach each one flush to the insides of the leg tops with 1 1/4" pocket screws, working in the same two-subassemblies-then-close order as the box. Before fastening, drill one extra pocket hole in the center of each apron facing UPWARD — that is what the tabletop screws into in the next step, and it is far easier to drill now on the bench than overhead later.

steps.title: Attach the tabletop
steps.body: Turn the flower top face down and set the base on it upside down, lining the outside corners of the legs up with the inside points between petals and shifting the base until every petal tip is the same distance from the leg corners — measure two opposite tips, then the other two, rather than trusting your eye. Mark the base position, then drive 1 1/4" screws up through the pocket holes in the aprons into the underside of the top. Turn it right side up: the legs are 20" and the top is 3/4", so the finished table stands about 20 3/4" tall. Sand every surface and edge smooth, round the petal edges thoroughly, and finish.

images.alt: Flower shaped kids play table with base storage
